summary: Cathy O'Neil examines shame as a social mechanism that operates in tension with free market ideology. She argues that shame traditionally functioned to get individuals to act against narrow self-interest for collective benefit, but our market-obsessed culture has created new scoring systems (dating apps, college rankings, social media metrics) that generate novel forms of shame. The piece uses the PUA/incel phenomenon as an example of how market logic applied to human relationships produces toxic outcomes. O'Neil suggests we've externalized and privatized traditional shaming mechanisms through technology and scoring systems, creating an overwhelming proliferation of ways to feel inadequate.
key_points: Shame traditionally serves a prosocial function—getting individuals to sacrifice self-interest for group benefit—but this only works when norms are reasonable and achievable
Market ideology and scoring systems have created new domains of shame (dating apps, college rankings, social metrics) that quantify human worth in reductive ways
The incel/PUA phenomenon exemplifies how market logic applied to dating produces dehumanizing frameworks and desperate attempts to conform
Understanding the flaws in scoring systems helps distance oneself from the shame they produce, but most people maintain 'blind faith' in these metrics
We've effectively externalized and privatized shame mechanisms through technology, replacing old notions of worth as family members and citizens with numerical scores
money_quotes: Shame often works really well to get individuals to act against their self interest in relatively small ways so that the group as a whole works more smoothly and is better off, at least ideally.

My theory is that, in part because of our weirdly religious belief in free markets, coupled with the explicitly market-flavored nature of dating apps and other technological intermediations on the sex and dating fronts, we end up with bizarre pseudo-scientific theories of attractions and attractiveness that purport to 'explain' everything about sex and dating but are fundamentally efforts to blunt the power of shame.
People really do care about rankings and scores, far beyond what I'd consider reasonable.

When you understand the flaws in scoring systems, you lose admiration for the system itself, which on the one hand helps you distance yourself from your score and any associated shame, and on the other hand allows you to see just how much blind faith and undue grief we as a society project onto such systems.
There's been a massive and largely undescribed conflict between the two systems of powers represented by the informal social mechanism of shame and more formal market mechanisms.

You could almost say that we've come to replace some of our old-fashioned notion of self-worth as family members, as citizens, and definitely as consumers by the scores that we've achieved. In that sense we've externalized and even privatized the dominant shaming mechanisms.
key_insight: The proliferation of scoring systems and market logic into every domain of life has transformed shame from an informal social mechanism for collective benefit into a privatized, externalized system of quantified inadequacy.
